Six lines of large Latin letters which reduce in height, with a vacat below.
Fine monumental letters with occasional serif flourishes, and some taller letters. Lightly incised hederae for interpuncts.
Usually thought either to refer to C.Maesius Titianus, cos. 245 CE, or a likely relative of similar period.
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Museo Civico Baldassare Romano
Fazello, 1558, F. Thomæ Fazelli... de rebus Siculis decades duæ

Honorific for Titianus on the occasion of his assumption of the toga virilis

Eight lines of continuous Latin text. Text fills most of stone, with stone-carver attempting to centre text onto stone. Upright hedera in line 4, with right-facing tail. Two final lines smaller.  Occasional use of interpuncts, in lines 1, 2, 5 and 6: tick-shaped in line 1, and more Y-shaped in subsequent lines. Some sort of interpunct/squiggle in line 8. Representing a pointed tilde (~)
Letters are tall and elongated. Ornate serifs used on every letter. Letters extremely regular and spacing between letters is consistent.
3rd century CE on prosopographical grounds (but difficult to pin down more tightly) - so too Barbieri
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 08-07-2022

Honorific for Marcus Cestius, primus pilus and duumvir
Siciliae obiacentium insular et Bruttiorum antiquae tabulae cum animadversionibus Georgii Gualtheri
G. Gualtherus, Siciliae obiacentium insular et Bruttiorum antiquae tabulae cum animadversionibus Georgii Gualtheri (Panormi, 1624)
Gualtherus presents the text over four lines, the name filling line 1.
No details recorded, other than that Gualtherus marks a tall I in line 2 ('pilo').
Most likely to be one of the first generation of colonial settlers, and so probably Augustan in date, and (as Wilson 1990, p.369 n.58) unlikely to be later than Claudius.
Wilson, 1990, Sicily under the Roman Empire: the archaeology of a Roman province, 36 B.C. - A.D. 535

Three lines of text filling the available field, the first line substantially larger than the second and third, with an attempt to centre the middle line.
Tall, slimly v-cut letters, with comma shaped interpuncts often enclosed in the letters due to cramping of the text. Substantial serifs on some letters in line 1.

two lines of Latin letters fill the available space, with the first line much larger than the second.
Large v-cut letters relatively wide in the module, with simple terminal serifs.

Fazello records hederae in lines 1 and 3, Gualtherus in line 1 only.
Gualtherus reports extensive but not entirely consistent use of hederae between words.
Funerary inscription for Publius Cestius Catullus
Four lines of Latin letters approximately centered on the stone with use of vacat in line 2 to render the text symmetrical.

Deep v-cut letters with minimal serifs. Interpuncts are clear deep round points, irregularly used. A has very light cross-bar. Reversed C in line two is much shallower than standard C. T on line 3 is tall; space without interpunct in line 3.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2022-07-08.
funerary inscription for publius cestius catullus
Presented by Gualtherus with the name in larger letters filling line 1, the epithet in smaller letters centred for line 2, and line 3 in smaller letters filling the width of the stone.
No information on letter forms preserved.
Four complete lines of Latin text approximately centred
Text is shallowly incised, irregular and rough. Letters are not straight, nor in line with each other. Very basic serifs, on occasional letters. Long tail on "L" in line 4.
Ferrua dates to the fourth century on the basis of the lettering.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2023-07-06.

Epitaph for Domitius Himeraeus, his sister Domitia and his wife Alfia Zotica
Three lines of roughly equal and centred latin letters filling the face of the stone, words separated by lightly carved interpuncts.
Simply cut reasonably regular letters, with one instance of ligature and an extended T in line 2.
Bivona suggests that the onomastics (presence of tribal, use of Aulus and lack of cognomen in the sister's name) imply a date in the first half of the C1 CE

Dedication of funerary plot for the parents of Cn. Domitius Piso

Only two lines of Latin text are preserved (two more below were previously observed). There is a large vacat (at least 47mm) above line 1, so it seems likely that there is no missing text above. Neat comma-shaped interpunct line 2.
Monumental letters, very straight and neatly carved, almost without serifs. Letters are widely spaced.
Irrecoverable height, only seen by Gualtherus

presented as two lines of Latin
Three lines of Latin text. Lines 1-2 are centred on the stone, line 3 is at the extreme left of the stone. Use of large 'T' shaped interpuncts.

Letters are deeply incised. Letters in lines 1-2 are relatively straight, equally spaced and keep to the line, whereas line 3 is a little more varied in all of these features. Large serifs are used, particularly in the tail of the 'R' in line 1, and that of the 'L' in line 2.

Inscribed statue base for Lucius Rubrius Proculus
Four lines of Latin text, centred on the stone and filling the face, slightly decreasing in size from line 1.

Large, plain, deeply cut letters. P is open, R and B are closed; some upright strokes (all letter I, some L and V) have a serif ascending upper right; plain interpuncts in lines 1 and 3; the numeral in line 3 is marked by a mid-bar.
Lettering and absence of filiation suggest a 2nd century CE date (Korhonen)

Boundary marker between Vandal and Goth territories
Likely erected in 500 CE, consequent upon dowry of Theoderic's sister's marriage with the Vandal king leading to transfer of region of Marsala into Vandal control.
boundary marker between vandal and goth territories
Fragment of a dedication to Venus Erycina

The text is arranged over nine lines. The first two lines (praescriptio), larger than the others, are placed across the top. Two columns follow (b and c), the first one consisting of two lines (and a vacat) and the second one of seven. Lines 1-2 of the left column form a separate text from lines 1-2 in the right column. The space between the columns is 2.4 cm.

Scriptura actuaria with letters made with the brush edge at an angle of less than 30 degrees to the writing line (rustica). All letters, except the last "a" in column 2, line 8, are rubricated, but most of the colour appears too bright to be ancient (and appears identical to that with which the inventory number on the lower left corner has been applied); it also appears that not all the interpuncts have been rubricated.

Funerary inscription for Marcus Limbricius and his wife Helvia Arura
Latin text preserved in full over four lines, vertically centred on the stone, with a vacat below. The first line is the largest, the third line smallest and most compressed.

The letters are large and simply cut, without serifs. M has hastae of equal length, all off-vertical; S is shallow; R is closed with a long tail extending from the eye; B has small upper eye, closed; V is wide; F has shorter lower bar; E has equal short bars; C is not perfectly curved/circular, but rather shallow. Interpuncts throughout, formed of light vertical strokes.

Four lines of Greek letters centred on the face of the stone, but with a leftward bias.

Very neatly carved wide, regular letters of a fairly square module, with minimal serifs and all letters of near equal size. Straight bar alpha; epsilon with only slightly shorter middle bar; mu with vertical first and last, middle strokes descen half-way; omicron near full size; pi has equal length verticals with slight overhang on both sides of the cross bar; rho has large rounded closed eye; sigma has horizontal top and bottom strokes; phi is circular and without over-extended vertical; omega is open and tear-drop shaped.
late second century BCE, or early C1 BCE, following Ampolo and Erdas (previously dated earlier by Nenci) and confirmed by parallel material

The text is set out over two lines on the lower part fo the front face of the slabe, in a field (4 cm high) demarcated from the upper part of the face by an incised horizontal line.

Small neat early Hellenistic letters, Alpha with straight bar, epsilon with short middle bar, zeta formed by vertical with horizontal bars top and bottom; Mu with four sloping strokes, full length; omicron, omega and theta all smaller than other letters; sigma with four equal strokes, top and bottom angled outwards; omega open with short horizontal tails.

Appears to have the distinctive Sicilian formula of 'chrestos kai amemptos', age in years and a family dedicant, indicating Roman imperial period.
Slightly unevenly laid out over 6 lines (Castelli provides an engraving, to emphasise that the presentation of Muratori is misleading)

Somewhat irregular and variable letters, thinly cut without serifs. alpha has broken bar; beta two approximately even, usually closed eyes; epsilon straight and bars of equal lengths; zeta has curving lower stroke; kappa has full length arms; mu has mid-strokes that start and finish below the top of the verticals and only descend partway; nu is reversed in line 6; rho is both angular and circular; sigma is lunate; omega is lunate.

Halaesa honours Diogenes Lapiron, son of Diogenes
Five lines of Greek text are preserved, with some of the initial letters lost at the left margin.
The letters are plainly and evenly cut, and well spaced, without serifs. Epsilon has a short middle bar, omicron is full size, omega is open.
The text cannot be precisely dated, but the style of the lettering suggests that it belongs to the 2nd century BCE, rather than the 1st.
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Museo Archeologico Regionale Antonino Salinas
halaesa honours diogenes lapiron, son of diogenes
A koinon of priests honours [-?-] Lapiron, son of Diogenes

Three lines of Greek text are preserved: the state of the stone means that the beginning and end of all three lines is lost, and at least one further line could have been lost below. If there were any lines above these, they would have been on a separate block of stone.

The letters are regularly cut, with deep terminations to the strokes rather than extended serifs. Epsilon has a shorter middle bar, detached from the main vertical; omicron is very slightly smaller than the other letters; omega is almost, or completely closed, with a slightly elliptical rather than purely circular form.
Scibona, 2008, La chiesa e il complesso edilizio postmedievale
Scibona, 2009, Decreto sacerdotale per il conferimento della euerghesia a Nemenios in Halaesa

Apollonia(?) honours Andron son of Thrasios
Four lines of Greek text, not quite centred, but lines 2 and 3 fill the width of the face.

Plain cut letters without serifs, of Hellenistic form. Alpha is straight bar (except possibly  broken bar in line 1); beta has two small closed eyes, with a gap between; epsilon has shorter middle bar; the form of theta is unclear (small circle or cross at centre?); kappa has small arms opening from the mid-point of the vertical; mu has curving middle strokes which reach down to the line, but vertical first and last; omicron is generally slightly smaller than the other letters; pi has equal length strokes without overlap; sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes; omega is closed and very slightly elongated, slightly smaller than other letters.
Photo J.Prag courtesy Soprintendenza BBCCAA di Messina
Salinas, 1876-, Notizie degli scavi di antichità
Bonanno, 2008, Apollonia. Indagini archeologiche sul Monte di San Fratello - Messina, 2003-2005
apollonia(?) honours andron son of thrasios
Ferrua, 1989, Note e giunte alle iscrizioni cristiane antiche della Sicilia
The municipium of the Haluntini honours Gnaeus Pollienus
The text occupies five lines over the upper part of the front face.

Regular letters with a fairly square module, simply engraved without serifs. Alpha has broken bar; Kappa has full length arms; Mu has vertical first and last, with mid-strokes only descending half-way; omicron is full size; Pi has full length verticals, and slightly overlapping horizontal bar; omega is slightly open and nearly circular, with horizontal tails/feet.

The use of the term 'municipium' in Greek places this text almost certainly after the grant of Latin rights to Sicily by Julius Caesar (probably 46 BCE), but equally the use of Greek implies a date not later than the early Augustan period. The lettering is compatible with a late Hellenistic date.

Α with a broken bar, lunate Ε and Σ, cursive M (at l. 2) and Ω, Ψ with a long upright. The cross bar of Η is formed decoratively. Interpuncts at ll. 1, 3 and 4, two ivy leaves at l. 4. Letters at ll. 4 and 5 are slightly larger and separated from the others by a larger space.

Irregular letters with serifs: Α with broken bar, lunate Ε and Σ, rhomboid Θ, Κ with long and curved upright, short arms, Μ with five slanting lines at l. 4, Ρ with the bottom of the eye left open, extended/tall iota. Ivy leaves at ll. 1, 3, 4 and 5, vertical bar between Γαμική and ἀνδρί at l. 6.
Letters with serifs, rhomboid Ο and Φ, angular cursive Ω at l. 5 (as a flipped Μ); ος at l. 2, τη at l. 3, ος and ρων at l. 5 in ligature. Triangle-shaped interpuncts at ll. 3, 4, 5, 6.
Lines 1, 4 and 7 are set further to the left, with the other lines indented by one character space, reflecting the beginning of each line of hexameter verse.
Regular letters: lunate Ε, Σ, cursive Ω, Α with broken bar.

Greek inscription recording enjoyment of a spring

The text maintains a clear left margin, laid out over three lines. The extended spacing in lines 1 and 2 has sometimes suggested to editors that the text divides into columns; however this is probably to compensate for the fact that lines 2 and 3 are shortened by the insertion of a large engraved palm leaf (20 cm high) on the right margin.

The text is engraved in large bold letters, with the topmost stroke often extended vertically above the letter on Α, Λ, Δ. Letter forms are somewhat variable within the inscription, with lunate and straight sigma used indiscriminately; epsilon is normally straight, but appears lunate in ΦΗϹΕΙΝΟϹ in line 2; alpha is normally straight but displays broken bar in ΕΥΦΡΑΝΘΗΣΑΝ in line 3; phi has an angular eye in lines 1 and 2 but a rounded eye in line 3; variation within texts is however common, and it is not necessary to assume that this reflects different hands in use within the text. Ligatures are employed several times in the text, which has been the source of several confused readings in the past.
3rd century CE or early 4th century CE (Manganaro)

greek inscription recording enjoyment of a spring

Three lines of large letters across the upper part of one of the wider faces. The inscribed field is approximately 0.36 m high x 0.30 m wide. The first line of text is less clear, especially around the upper margin and at the right edge. A continuous horizontal line separates lines 1 and 2.

The letters are engraved in broad, rounded strokes (partly the result of weathering) and have a regular form. K has arms that do not quite reach to the top and bottom of the letter; E has strokes of equal length; A has broken bar. The text is either Latin or a mixture or Latin and Greek, given the presence of L in the second line.
